chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Orthorhombic structure isostructural with barite (BaSO4). Sr occupies 12-coordinate sites. Complete solid solution toward barite (BaSO4) and incomplete solution toward anglesite (PbSO4). The pure Sr endmember is the mineral 'celestite' (also called 'celestine').
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Pure SrSO4 is colorless to white; the characteristic pale blue color comes from color centers related to trace impurities or radiation effects. Strontium is one of the elements that gives a brilliant crimson red in flame tests and fireworks. Celestite is the primary commercial source of strontium compounds.

crystallography.crystallography_notes: Commonly forms well-developed tabular or prismatic orthorhombic crystals. Tabular {001} with prominent pinacoidal faces typical. Also fibrous, granular, and massive forms. Geode fillings in limestone (Madagascan geodes) are the most famous collector form — clusters of blue crystals in hollow limestone cavities.

physical_properties.physical_notes: High SG for a non-metallic light-colored mineral (nearly 4.0). Perfect basal cleavage. Soluble in concentrated H2SO4. Flame test: strontium produces vivid crimson-red flame — useful field test.

color_profiles.name: Blue Celestite
color_profiles.cause: Color centers from trace impurities and/or radiation effects; mechanism not fully established.
color_profiles.conditions: Sedimentary and evaporitic deposits worldwide; most famous from Madagascar geodes.

color_profiles.name: Colorless / White Celestite
color_profiles.conditions: Common in evaporitic and hydrothermal settings.

color_profiles.name: Orange / Yellow Celestite
color_profiles.cause: Iron oxide staining or trace impurities.

common_forms: tabular crystal clusters in limestone geode cavities (Madagascar)
appearance.visual_notes: Madagascar celestite geodes — hollow limestone nodules lined with pale-blue tabular crystals — are among the most widely sold mineral specimens globally. The celestial blue color is gentle and consistent. Crystals often have a translucent quality with pearly luster on cleavage surfaces.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Some specimens fluoresce pale blue or white under SW UV
optical_and_special_effects.chatoyancy: Fibrous material may show weak cat's eye
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Biaxial positive. RI approximately 1.619–1.631. Low birefringence. Pearly cleavage luster from structural reflection.

notable_localities: Sakoany, Mahajanga (Majunga), Madagascar (world's premier source of geode specimens)
Put-in-Bay (South Bass Island), Lake Erie, Ohio, USA (historically important)
Strontian, Argyllshire, Scotland (historical — strontium named here)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Celestite is the primary ore of strontium. Madagascar's Sakoany deposit produces spectacular geodes and is the world's most important source for collector material. The mineral 'strontianite' (SrCO3) and celestite were both first found near Strontian, Scotland — giving the element strontium its name. Industrial celestite mining in Poland, Germany, and Spain produces strontium for fireworks, glass TV tubes (historical), and medical uses.

historical_uses: Primary strontium ore — industrial source of strontium compounds
Strontium for red traffic lights and signal flares
Strontium carbonate for CRT television glass (historical)
cultural_associations: Name from Latin 'caelestis' (heavenly) — referring to celestial-blue color
Madagascar geode trade sustains local mining communities
Common in crystal healing market — 'stone of celestial communication'
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: The name 'celestite' or 'celestine' comes from Latin 'caelestis' (heavenly) — a reference to the pale sky-blue color. The element strontium was first isolated from celestite and strontianite found near Strontian, a village in Argyll, Scotland — giving the element its name. Madagascar's Sakoany celestite geodes have supplied the global collector market for decades and are among the most recognizable mineral specimens in gift shops worldwide.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Celestite holds the color of open sky inside a limestone cave. The name is fitting — pale blue, pearly, translucent, quiet. Madagascar geodes are small sky-caverns. A gentle mineral with a celestial aspiration.

practical_notes.toxicity: Low toxicity. Strontium in mineral form is relatively insoluble and not hazardous. Strontium salts in solution have some mild health concerns but not relevant to normal specimen handling.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Soft (Mohs 3–3.5) and brittle with perfect cleavage — handle crystal specimens carefully. Heavy for apparent size. Geode specimens need protection for crystal points.
practical_notes.care_notes: Avoid water immersion (partially soluble over time). Clean with dry or barely damp soft brush. Do not expose to acids. Handle with care to avoid cleavage damage.
treatments_or_synthetics: Dyeing of pale specimens to enhance blue color (uncommon)
common_confusions: Barite (BaSO4 — higher SG; similar habit but heavier)
Blue calcite (effervesces in HCl; cubic cleavage)
practical_notes.market_notes: Affordable collector mineral. Madagascar geodes widely available at low prices. Larger crystals and exceptional specimens from Sicily and Ohio command moderate premium. Industrial material is commodity (strontium ore).

prompt_fragments: Madagascar celestite geode — hollow limestone cave of sky-blue tabular crystals
pale celestial-blue orthorhombic clusters — heaven's palette in strontium sulfate
pearly-blue celestite tabular crystal with pearlescent cleavage
